message.content:
Adversaries may exploit the "Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Login Items" technique to gain persistence or escalate privileges by adding login items that execute upon user login. These items can be applications, documents, folders, or server connections that launch automatically. They can be added via a shared file list or the Service Management Framework. Shared file list items are visible in System Preferences and can be manipulated using AppleScript, while Service Management Framework items are hidden and managed through the SMLoginItemSetEnabled API call.

The potential impact includes unauthorized execution of malicious code, remote system control, and privilege escalation. Common exploitation methods involve using AppleScript and Native API calls to create login items that spawn malicious executables. Notable malware such as Dok, Green Lambert, and NETWIRE have utilized this technique for persistence.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting the use of AppleScript and Native API calls, regularly auditing login items in System Preferences, and employing endpoint security solutions to detect and block unauthorized login item modifications. Additionally, user education on recognizing and reporting unusual login items can help mitigate this threat.
